Description: Domain Controller health and performance checks

Checks.PassMessage: All critical AD services are running on all DCs
Checks.FailMessage: Critical AD services are not running

EvaluationRules.Message: Critical AD service is stopped on a domain controller
Critical service issue detected on domain controller

Checks.Help: Verifies that essential AD-related Windows services are running on each DC (NTDS, NETLOGON, DNS, KDC, W32Time, etc.). Stopped critical services directly impact authentication and replication.
Checks.Remediation: 1. Identify stopped services: Get-Service NTDS,NETLOGON,DNS,KDC,W32Time | Where-Object {$_.Status -ne 'Running'}. 2. Start stopped services: Start-Service <ServiceName>. 3. Set startup type to Automatic if needed: Set-Service <ServiceName> -StartupType Automatic. 4. If a service fails to start, check Event Viewer Application and System logs for errors.

Checks.PassMessage: Disk space is adequate on all domain controllers
Checks.FailMessage: Low disk space detected on domain controller

EvaluationRules.Message: Critical disk space shortage on domain controller
Low disk space on domain controller
Disk space issue detected on domain controller

Checks.Help: Monitors free disk space on all DC volumes, particularly the NTDS database and SYSVOL volumes. Insufficient disk space can cause AD to stop accepting writes and prevent backup completion.
Checks.Remediation: 1. Identify large files consuming space with: Get-ChildItem C:\ -Recurse | Sort-Object Length -Descending | Select-Object -First 20. 2. Clean up old log files, temp files, and Windows Update cache (cleanmgr). 3. If NTDS volume is full, perform offline defragmentation to recover white space. 4. Consider expanding the volume or moving NTDS to a larger disk.

Checks.FailMessage: One or more domain controllers are unreachable

EvaluationRules.Message: Domain controller is unreachable
DC reachability issue detected

Checks.Help: Verifies that all domain controllers are reachable via network connectivity tests. Unreachable DCs cannot service authentication requests or participate in replication.
Checks.Remediation: 1. Verify the DC is powered on and network cables are connected. 2. Check firewall rules - AD requires TCP/UDP 389, 636, 3268, 445, 135, 49152-65535. 3. Verify DNS resolution for the DC's FQDN. 4. Check if the NETLOGON and Active Directory Domain Services services are running.

Checks.PassMessage: CPU utilization is within normal limits on all DCs
Checks.FailMessage: High CPU utilization detected on domain controller

EvaluationRules.Message: Critical CPU utilization on domain controller - performance severely impacted
High CPU utilization on domain controller
CPU utilization issue detected on domain controller

Checks.Help: Monitors CPU usage on each DC. Sustained high CPU on a DC causes slow authentication, LDAP query timeouts, and replication delays.
Checks.Remediation: 1. Identify top CPU-consuming processes with Task Manager or: Get-Process | Sort-Object CPU -Descending | Select-Object -First 10. 2. Check for runaway antivirus scans - exclude NTDS.dit and SYSVOL from real-time scanning. 3. Check for LDAP query storms with: netstat -an | findstr :389. 4. Consider adding additional DCs to distribute the authentication load.

Checks.PassMessage: Memory utilization is within normal limits on all DCs
Checks.FailMessage: Memory pressure detected on domain controller

EvaluationRules.Message: Critical memory pressure on domain controller
Low available memory on domain controller
Memory pressure issue detected on domain controller

Checks.Help: Checks available memory on each DC. Low memory causes LSASS paging, slow LDAP responses, and increased authentication latency across the environment.
Checks.Remediation: 1. Identify memory consumers: Get-Process | Sort-Object WorkingSet -Descending | Select-Object -First 10. 2. Ensure LSASS is not being paged - check Performance Monitor for 'Memory\Pages/sec'. 3. Disable unnecessary services and roles on the DC. 4. Add RAM to the server or migrate roles to reduce memory pressure.

Checks.PassMessage: Disk IO latency is within acceptable limits on all DCs
Checks.FailMessage: High disk IO latency detected on domain controller

EvaluationRules.Message: Critical disk IO latency on domain controller - AD operations severely impacted
Elevated disk IO latency on domain controller
Disk IO latency issue detected

Checks.Help: Monitors disk I/O latency on DC volumes, particularly where NTDS.dit resides. High latency (over 20ms) on the AD database disk causes slow LDAP responses and authentication delays.
Checks.Remediation: 1. Identify high-latency disks with Performance Monitor counters: LogicalDisk\Avg. Disk sec/Read and Write. 2. Ensure NTDS.dit is on a dedicated volume, not sharing with OS or page file. 3. Check for disk fragmentation and defragment if needed. 4. Consider moving NTDS.dit to faster storage (SSD). 5. Exclude NTDS.dit and logs from antivirus real-time scanning.

Checks.PassMessage: LDAP response times are within acceptable limits on all DCs
Checks.FailMessage: Slow LDAP response time detected on domain controller

EvaluationRules.Message: Critical LDAP response time - authentication and directory queries severely degraded
Slow LDAP response time on domain controller
LDAP response time issue detected

Checks.Help: Tests LDAP connectivity and response time to each DC on ports 389 (LDAP) and 636 (LDAPS). Slow or failed LDAP responses indicate DC overload or network issues affecting all AD-dependent applications.
Checks.Remediation: 1. Check DC CPU and memory utilization (see DC-004, DC-005). 2. Test LDAP manually: ldp.exe or Test-NetConnection -ComputerName DC01 -Port 389. 3. Review LDAP query efficiency - expensive queries appear in Event ID 1644 in the Directory Service log. 4. Enable LDAP diagnostic logging to identify problematic clients.

Checks.PassMessage: Kerberos KDC service is running normally on all DCs
Checks.FailMessage: Kerberos functionality issue detected on domain controller

EvaluationRules.Message: KDC service is not running - Kerberos authentication will fail
Elevated Kerberos errors in the last 24 hours
Kerberos functionality issue detected

Checks.Help: Tests end-to-end Kerberos authentication by requesting a service ticket to each DC. Kerberos failures affect all domain-joined computers and cause widespread authentication errors.
Checks.Remediation: 1. Check time synchronization - Kerberos requires clocks within 5 minutes: w32tm /stripchart /computer:<DC>. 2. Verify the KDC service is running: Get-Service KDC. 3. Check DNS name resolution for the DC. 4. Review KDC errors in Event Viewer Security log. 5. Ensure the krbtgt account password is not corrupted.

Checks.PassMessage: Global Catalog servers are responding normally

EvaluationRules.Message: Global Catalog server is not responding on port 3268
Global Catalog server is not advertising GC role
Global Catalog availability issue detected

Checks.Help: Verifies that each DC configured as a Global Catalog is advertising the GC service via DNS. Missing GC SRV records prevent cross-domain lookups and Universal Group membership resolution.
Checks.Remediation: 1. On the DC run: nltest /dsgetdc:<domain> /gc to verify GC availability. 2. If GC is not advertising, restart the NTDS service: Stop-Service NTDS -Force; Start-Service NTDS. 3. Verify GC configuration in Active Directory Sites and Services. 4. Check DNS for _gc._tcp SRV records under the forest root zone.

Checks.PassMessage: No certificates expiring soon on domain controllers
Checks.FailMessage: Certificates expiring soon detected on domain controllers

EvaluationRules.Message: Domain controller certificates expiring within 30 days
Domain controller certificates expiring within 90 days
Certificate expiration issue detected

Checks.Help: Checks SSL/TLS certificates on each DC for upcoming expiration. Expired certificates cause LDAPS connection failures and may break smart card authentication and PKI-dependent services.
Checks.Remediation: 1. Identify expiring certificates: Get-ChildItem Cert:\LocalMachine\My | Where-Object {$_.NotAfter -lt (Get-Date).AddDays(30)}. 2. If using an internal CA, request a new certificate from your CA. 3. If using auto-enrollment, check the Certificate Templates and GPO enrollment settings. 4. After renewal, verify LDAPS works: Test-NetConnection -ComputerName DC01 -Port 636.

Checks.PassMessage: System event logs show no excessive errors on domain controllers
Checks.FailMessage: Elevated error counts detected in System event log

EvaluationRules.Message: Critical-level events found in System event log in last 24 hours
More than 50 error events in System event log in last 24 hours
More than 20 error events in System event log in last 24 hours
Event log error count issue detected on domain controller

Checks.Help: Scans the System event log on each DC for critical and error-level events in the past 24 hours. High error counts indicate underlying hardware, driver, or service issues that need investigation.
Checks.Remediation: 1. Review critical events in Event Viewer: System log, Level = Critical or Error. 2. Common sources: disk errors (source: disk), network errors (source: NDIS), service crashes (source: Service Control Manager). 3. Search the Event ID on Microsoft Learn for specific guidance. 4. Address hardware warnings promptly - failing disks or memory can corrupt the AD database.

Checks.PassMessage: All network adapters are connected on domain controllers
Checks.FailMessage: Network adapter connectivity issues detected on domain controller

EvaluationRules.Message: A physical network adapter is not connected on a domain controller

Checks.Help: Checks that all enabled network adapters on each DC show as connected. A disconnected adapter can isolate the DC from clients or replication partners depending on the network layout.
Checks.Remediation: 1. Check physical cable connections on the server. 2. Verify NIC configuration: Get-NetAdapter | Where-Object {$_.Status -ne 'Up'}. 3. Check for driver issues in Device Manager. 4. If using NIC teaming, verify team member status. 5. Check switch port configuration for the DC's port.

Checks.PassMessage: LSASS memory usage is within normal limits on all DCs
Checks.FailMessage: High LSASS memory usage detected on domain controller

EvaluationRules.Message: LSASS is using more than 2 GB of memory - possible memory leak
LSASS memory usage exceeds 1 GB - monitor for memory leak
LSASS memory usage issue detected

Checks.Help: Monitors memory consumption of the LSASS process, which handles all AD authentication. Excessive LSASS memory (over 1GB) indicates a large AD database, many trusts, or a potential memory leak.
Checks.Remediation: 1. If LSASS exceeds 2GB, check for LSASS memory leaks in recent Windows updates. 2. Large LSASS memory is normal on DCs with millions of objects - add RAM. 3. Reduce the number of trusts if possible. 4. Apply Windows security patches - some versions have LSASS memory leak fixes. 5. Consider enabling Credential Guard to reduce LSASS attack surface.
